children.name: Leiurus quinquestriatus
children.description: The world's most dangerous scorpion; responsible for 75% of scorpion-related deaths in North Africa. Venom chlorotoxin now investigated for brain tumour imaging and therapy.

children.name: Leiurus hebraeus
children.description: Found in Israel and the surrounding Levant region; closely related to L. quinquestriatus and similarly venomous. Occupies rocky desert scrubland and is considered medically significant.

children.name: Leiurus abdullahbayrami
children.description: Described from southeastern Turkey; one of the more recently recognised Leiurus species, distinguished from relatives by morphological and molecular characters. Inhabits rocky limestone hillsides and is considered medically important in its range.

children.name: Leiurus haenggii
children.description: Known from Egypt and the eastern Saharan fringe; separated from L. quinquestriatus on the basis of subtle morphological differences and geographic isolation. Venom composition is closely comparable to other Leiurus species and is medically significant.

children.name: Leiurus jordanensis
children.description: Endemic to Jordan; occupies arid rocky wadis and desert margins, typically under stones and in crevices. Closely related to L. quinquestriatus, sharing highly toxic neurotoxic venom that poses a genuine medical risk.

children.name: Leiurus lucifugus
children.description: Recorded from Libya and adjacent North African desert; the epithet refers to its strongly light-avoiding, nocturnal behaviour. Venom potency is comparable to congeners and warrants medical attention following envenomation.

children.name: Leiurus savanicola
children.description: Described from Sudan and adjacent East Africa; inhabits open savanna and rocky escarpments at the southern margin of the Sahara. Venom is highly neurotoxic, comparable to other Leiurus species, and envenomation requires prompt medical attention.

children.name: Leiurus arabicus
children.description: Found across the Arabian Peninsula in sandy and gravelly desert habitats.

children.name: Leiurus niloticus
children.description: Recorded from the Nile Valley and Delta of Egypt; favours irrigated agricultural margins.

children.name: Leiurus somalicus
children.description: From the Horn of Africa, extending through arid lowlands of Somalia and eastern Ethiopia.

children.name: Leiurus atratus
children.description: An unusually dark Leiurus from the highlands of Yemen and southwestern Saudi Arabia above 2000 m.

children.name: Leiurus venator
children.description: The westernmost Leiurus in Africa, from the Sahelian savanna belt of West Africa.

children.name: Androctonus australis
children.description: Most deadly scorpion in North Africa by fatalities. Markedly thickened metasoma. Sting causes cardiovascular collapse.

children.name: Androctonus crassicauda
children.description: Most dangerous scorpion in the Middle East. Venom contains potent cardiotoxins and neurotoxins.

children.name: Androctonus amoreuxi
children.description: Widespread across North Africa and the Arabian Peninsula; its venom is potent though sting mortality is lower than A. australis. Favours sandy and rocky desert habitats.

children.name: Androctonus mauritanicus
children.description: Native to Morocco and western Algeria; venom is highly toxic and responsible for significant human envenomations in the Maghreb region. Large and robust body with a characteristically thick tail.

children.name: Androctonus bicolor
children.description: Distributed from Egypt through the Arabian Peninsula; distinctive two-tone coloration with a dark metasoma. Delivers highly toxic venom that can cause severe envenomation symptoms in humans.

children.name: Androctonus finitimus
children.description: Occurs across northeastern Africa and into the Arabian Peninsula, inhabiting arid sandy and stony desert terrain. Its venom is neurotoxic and considered medically significant, though less studied than that of close relatives.

children.name: Androctonus hoggarensis
children.description: Endemic to the Hoggar (Ahaggar) Mountains of southern Algeria; adapted to extreme highland desert conditions with large diurnal temperature swings. Represents an isolated population with distinct morphological characters from lowland Androctonus relatives.

children.name: Androctonus sergenti
children.description: Described from Algeria; inhabits semi-arid and desert zones of the central Maghreb. Venom composition has not been fully characterised but the species is presumed medically relevant given its genus affiliation.

children.name: Androctonus liouvillei
children.description: Known from Morocco and Algeria; a moderately sized Androctonus with neurotoxic venom typical of the genus. Occupies rocky hillsides and desert scrub, active nocturnally in the warmer months.

children.name: Androctonus sistani
children.description: Described from the Sistan-Baluchestan region of southeastern Iran; among the easternmost Androctonus species, reflecting the genus's extension into Central Asia. Inhabits hot, arid wind-swept plains and is considered a public health concern in rural communities.

children.name: Androctonus gonneti
children.description: Found in the Atlas Mountains; a high-elevation species experiencing cold winters.

children.name: Androctonus aequalis
children.description: Distributed across the Sahel from Mali to Sudan with symmetrical carinal granulation.

children.name: Androctonus amoreuxi tunetanus
children.description: A Tunisian subspecies inhabiting sandy coastal plains and oases.

children.name: Androctonus euphrates
children.description: An Iraqi and Syrian species tied to alluvial soils of the Euphrates river valley.

children.name: Androctonus degrandense
children.description: A recently described species from the Hoggar Mountains of southern Algeria.

children.name: Centruroides sculpturatus
children.description: The only scorpion in North America dangerous to healthy adults. Hides under bark; can climb smooth vertical surfaces. Glows bright blue-green under UV.

children.name: Centruroides exilicauda
children.description: Closely related to C. sculpturatus; found in the Sonoran Desert.

children.name: Centruroides vittatus
children.description: The most common scorpion in the United States, ranging across the central and southern states. Sting is painful but rarely life-threatening to healthy adults; responsible for more stings in the USA than any other species.

children.name: Centruroides limpidus
children.description: Found in western and central Mexico; one of the more medically significant Centruroides species, with venom causing severe neurotoxic symptoms. Inhabits dry tropical forests and rocky hillsides.

children.name: Centruroides noxius
children.description: Endemic to Nayarit state in Mexico and considered the most dangerous Centruroides species; historically responsible for numerous deaths before antivenoms were widely available. Its neurotoxic venom can cause respiratory failure.

children.name: Centruroides suffusus
children.description: Endemic to the Durango region of Mexico; one of the most venomous North American scorpions, with venom producing severe neurotoxic effects. Antivenom development for this species contributed significantly to scorpion toxinology research.

children.name: Centruroides gracilis
children.description: Widespread from Mexico through Central America and into South America, and introduced to Florida; highly adaptable to disturbed habitats and a frequent synanthropic species. Sting is painful with neurotoxic effects but rarely life-threatening to healthy adults.

children.name: Centruroides margaritatus
children.description: Ranges from western Mexico through Central America into northwestern South America; a common arboreal species sheltering under bark and in bromeliads. Venom is neurotoxic but sting mortality is low; well-known in the exotic pet trade.

children.name: Centruroides nigrescens
children.description: Found in Colombia and Panama; distinguished by its notably dark, almost black coloration compared to other Centruroides. Inhabits humid tropical forest and is associated with dead wood and bark substrates.

children.name: Centruroides ornatus
children.description: Native to the Pacific coast of Mexico, particularly Jalisco and Colima states; a beautifully marked species with contrasting pale and dark bands. Venom is neurotoxic and envenomation can produce severe systemic effects comparable to other medically important Mexican Centruroides.

children.name: Centruroides infamatus
children.description: Occurring in the highland regions of central Mexico; a medically important species whose venom is highly neurotoxic and has been responsible for human deaths. Antivenom research using this species contributed substantially to the development of Mexican polyvalent scorpion antivenom.

children.name: Centruroides tecomanus
children.description: Endemic to a restricted area of Colima state in western Mexico; among the most locally dangerous scorpions in its range with exceptionally potent neurotoxic venom. Inhabits tropical dry forest and agricultural margins.

children.name: Centruroides elegans
children.description: Found in central and southern Mexico with contrasting black-and-yellow banded legs.

children.name: Centruroides bernoulli
children.description: A South American Centruroides from Colombia and Venezuela.

children.name: Centruroides flaccidus
children.description: A Mexican species from the Balsas Basin with relatively less potent venom.

children.name: Tityus serrulatus
children.description: Responsible for the most serious scorpion envenomations in South America; over 300,000 stings annually in Brazil. Parthenogenetic -- females reproduce without males.

children.name: Tityus bahiensis
children.description: Second most medically important scorpion in Brazil after T. serrulatus; dark coloration and widespread across southeastern Brazil. Envenomation can cause severe systemic symptoms, particularly dangerous to children.

children.name: Tityus stigmurus
children.description: Common in northeastern Brazil; a public health concern in urban and periurban environments due to its adaptability to human-modified habitats. Venom causes pain, sweating, and in severe cases cardiovascular complications.

children.name: Tityus obscurus
children.description: Widely distributed across the Amazon basin; the dominant medically significant scorpion of Amazonia. Envenomation produces distinctive neurological symptoms including visual disturbances and convulsions rarely seen with other Tityus species.

children.name: Tityus trivittatus
children.description: The most medically important scorpion in Argentina, responsible for the majority of serious envenomations and deaths in that country. Urban-adapted and parthenogenetic, it thrives in city gardens, rubble, and domestic environments.

children.name: Tityus costatus
children.description: Found in southeastern Brazil; characterised by pronounced longitudinal ridging on the metasoma segments. Venom is medically relevant and capable of producing severe systemic envenomation, especially in children.

children.name: Tityus cambridgei
children.description: Distributed across the Guiana Shield and adjacent Amazon regions; a forest-floor species associated with leaf litter and fallen logs. Venom composition includes novel peptides under investigation for pharmacological properties.

children.name: Tityus clathratus
children.description: Occurs in Venezuela and Trinidad; named for its distinctive latticed body patterning of pale and dark markings. Medically important within its range; venom causes intense pain and cardiovascular symptoms.

children.name: Tityus magnimanus
children.description: Found in Brazil and Paraguay; notable for its proportionally enlarged chelae relative to other Tityus species, suggesting a more prehensile predatory strategy. Venom is neurotoxic and can produce severe envenomation symptoms.

children.name: Tityus neglectus
children.description: Described from northeastern Brazil; long overlooked taxonomically, hence the name, but now recognised as a distinct and medically relevant species. Envenomation records indicate potentially severe systemic effects.

children.name: Tityus pusillus
children.description: One of the smallest Tityus species, found in northeastern Brazil; despite its diminutive size its venom is neurotoxic and can cause significant local and systemic effects. Often found in leaf litter, bromeliads, and under bark in humid coastal forests.

children.name: Tityus silvestris
children.description: A forest-dwelling species ranging across the Amazon basin and Guiana highlands; typically found under bark and in tree cavities. Envenomation produces neurological symptoms and has been responsible for deaths in rural Amazonia.

children.name: Tityus metuendus
children.description: Distributed across the western Amazon in Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia; the name means 'to be feared', reflecting its potent venom. Envenomation can provoke severe cardiovascular and neurological effects and is considered a significant public health risk in remote Amazonian communities.

children.name: Tityus falcifer
children.description: Named for the sickle-shaped hook on the telson; part of the T. fasciolatus complex.

children.name: Tityus guayakum
children.description: Found in the dry Chaco forests of Paraguay and Bolivia; aestivates in deep burrows.

children.name: Tityus spurrelli
children.description: A Colombian forest species with reddish pedipalps.

children.name: Hottentotta
children.description: Large genus of Old World buthid scorpions ranging from Africa through South Asia; includes the medically critical Indian red scorpion.

children.name: Hottentotta tamulus
children.description: Considered the most lethal scorpion in the world by case fatality rate, with mortality reaching 30% in untreated children in parts of India. Its venom triggers massive sympathetic activation causing pulmonary edema and cardiac failure.

children.name: Hottentotta hottentotta
children.description: Widespread across sub-Saharan Africa; a large, aggressive buthid whose venom is medically significant, particularly to children and elderly. Favours savanna and dry woodland habitats.

children.name: Hottentotta jayakari
children.description: Found in Oman, Yemen, and adjacent parts of the Arabian Peninsula; inhabits rocky and gravelly desert terrain at various elevations. Its neurotoxic venom is medically relevant and the species is an important component of Arabian arachnofauna.

children.name: Hottentotta conspersus
children.description: Distributed across southern and eastern Africa in savanna and thornbush environments; characterised by its pale, speckled coloration providing strong camouflage. Venom is neurotoxic and sting is considered medically important.

children.name: Hottentotta franzwerneri
children.description: Recorded from North Africa and the Middle East; a relatively slender Hottentotta with yellowish-brown coloration adapted to arid landscapes. Associated with sandy and stony desert habitats where it shelters under rocks and debris.

children.name: Hottentotta salei
children.description: Described from Ethiopia and adjacent East Africa; inhabits arid savanna and semi-desert zones with scattered rocky outcrops. Venom is neurotoxic; medically important in the rural communities within its range.

children.name: Hottentotta caboverdensis
children.description: Endemic to the Cape Verde archipelago; an island-dwelling species that arrived via natural dispersal or ancient human introduction from mainland West Africa. Occupies rocky and coastal habitats on several islands, with venom consistent with other Hottentotta species.

children.name: Hottentotta scaber
children.description: Found across Pakistan, Afghanistan, and northwestern India; inhabits semi-arid plains and rocky foothills. Its roughened, granular cuticle gives it the epithet 'scaber'; venom is neurotoxic and the species is medically significant across its South Asian range.

children.name: Hottentotta trilineatus
children.description: Widespread in South Asia from India through Myanmar; characterised by three pale longitudinal stripes on the mesosoma. Frequently encountered in agricultural and periurban areas; venom is medically important and envenomation can cause serious systemic effects.

children.name: Hottentotta saulcyi
children.description: Distributed across Turkey, Syria, and Iraq; one of the more northerly Hottentotta species and able to tolerate a wider range of temperatures than its tropical relatives. Envenomation is medically important; inhabits rocky hillsides and steppe.

children.name: Hottentotta gentili
children.description: Found in Somalia and the Horn of Africa; inhabits rocky semi-arid lowlands subject to seasonal rains. Venom composition and medical significance are comparable to related East African Hottentotta species.

children.name: Hottentotta leptochelys
children.description: A slender Hottentotta from the Ethiopian highlands with unusually narrow pedipalps.

children.name: Hottentotta alburquerquensis
children.description: From Gujarat, India; inhabits thorn scrub and arid agricultural land.

children.name: Hottentotta spiniger
children.description: Distributed across the Horn of Africa with notably spiny pedipalp segments.

children.name: Parabuthus
children.description: African genus of robust, highly venomous scorpions; several species can spray venom as a defensive mechanism.

children.name: Parabuthus transvaalicus
children.description: One of Africa's most dangerous scorpions; capable of spraying venom up to 1 metre as a defence. Found in southern Africa from South Africa through Zimbabwe, its neurotoxic venom can cause respiratory failure.

children.name: Parabuthus villosus
children.description: The largest and one of the most venomous scorpions in southern Africa, found in Namibia and South Africa. Distinctive hairy appearance and massive chelae; able to spray venom defensively and delivers a sting potentially fatal to humans.

children.name: Parabuthus leiosoma
children.description: Distributed from the Horn of Africa through Yemen and Oman; one of the more widespread Parabuthus species, occupying arid coastal plains and rocky escarpments. Venom is potently neurotoxic; the epithet 'leiosoma' refers to its relatively smooth body surface compared to hairier congeners.

children.name: Parabuthus mossambicensis
children.description: Found in Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and parts of Tanzania; inhabits coastal lowland savanna and dry woodland. Venom is neurotoxic and the species is medically significant; accounts for a portion of scorpion envenomations in the region.

children.name: Parabuthus pallidus
children.description: Endemic to Somalia and the Ogaden region; pale coloration provides camouflage in sandy and light-coloured substrate habitats. Venom is neurotoxic and medically relevant in the remote communities of the Horn of Africa.

children.name: Parabuthus granulatus
children.description: Widespread in southern Africa, particularly the arid Karoo and Namib-adjacent zones of South Africa and Namibia; the heavily granulated cuticle gives this species its name. Capable of venom spraying; sting produces potent neurotoxic effects and is considered life-threatening.

children.name: Parabuthus capensis
children.description: Restricted to the Western Cape and Northern Cape provinces of South Africa; adapted to the Mediterranean-type fynbos and succulent Karoo biomes. Highly venomous; venom contains potent neurotoxins and the species is responsible for serious envenomations in the region.

children.name: Parabuthus namibiensis
children.description: Endemic to the Namib Desert sand seas; pale yellow to match the dune sand.

children.name: Parabuthus karooensis
children.description: A small Parabuthus from the Succulent Karoo biome of South Africa.

children.name: Buthus
children.description: Mediterranean and North African genus; the type genus of the family Buthidae.

children.name: Buthus occitanus
children.description: The most widespread scorpion in Europe, found across the Mediterranean basin from Spain to Turkey and into North Africa. Its venom potency varies greatly by population; North African and Middle Eastern populations are significantly more dangerous than European ones.

children.name: Buthus algerianus
children.description: The most widespread Buthus in Algeria and Tunisia; venom shows regional variation.

children.name: Buthus montanus
children.description: A high-elevation Buthus from the Sierra Nevada of Spain above 2000 m.

children.name: Buthus maroccanus
children.description: Endemic to the Rif and Middle Atlas mountains of Morocco.

children.name: Mesobuthus
children.description: Diverse genus of buthid scorpions distributed from the Mediterranean eastward to China; several species are medically important.

children.name: Mesobuthus eupeus
children.description: One of the most widespread scorpions in the Middle East and Central Asia, extending into southeastern Europe. A frequent cause of human envenomation across its range, with venom producing intense pain and systemic effects.

children.name: Mesobuthus martensii
children.description: The most commercially harvested scorpion in the world; used extensively in traditional Chinese medicine for millennia. Its venom peptides are researched for analgesic and anti-tumour applications, and the species is farmed at industrial scale in China.

children.name: Mesobuthus gibbosus
children.description: The only scorpion native to Greece and the Balkans, extending through Anatolia; one of the northernmost Mesobuthus species. Inhabits dry rocky hillsides with sparse vegetation; sting is painful but mortality in healthy adults is low.

children.name: Mesobuthus caucasicus
children.description: Distributed across the Caucasus region and into Central Asia; tolerates a wide temperature range for a buthid. Venom is neurotoxic; medically relevant in rural communities of Georgia, Armenia, Azerbaijan, and adjacent countries.

children.name: Mesobuthus ternerii
children.description: From the Kyzylkum Desert of Uzbekistan; adapted to continental climate extremes.

children.name: Mesobuthus balticus
children.description: The northernmost Mesobuthus, found around the Caspian Sea and Volga Delta.

children.name: Isometrus
children.description: Pan-tropical genus of slender buthid scorpions; among the most widely distributed scorpions globally due to accidental human transport.

children.name: Isometrus maculatus
children.description: The world's most widely distributed scorpion, having spread throughout the tropics and subtropics via human commerce. Frequents human dwellings; sting is painful but not life-threatening to healthy adults.

children.name: Isometrus granulatus
children.description: Found across South and Southeast Asia and into Australasia; a synanthropic species commonly encountered in buildings and under debris in gardens. Sting produces localised pain and mild systemic symptoms; generally not life-threatening.

children.name: Isometrus thurstoni
children.description: Native to the Fijian archipelago; one of the few Isometrus species with a relatively restricted island distribution rather than broad pantropical spread. Inhabits coastal forest and scrub; venom is mild and sting is of little medical significance.

children.name: Isometrus heimi
children.description: Described from India and Sri Lanka; a slender, small Isometrus associated with forest-edge and cultivated habitats. Venom is of low medical significance; the species is part of the taxonomically complex Indo-Pacific Isometrus fauna.

children.name: Isometrus litoralis
children.description: Found along coastlines of the Indian Ocean islands and adjacent mainland shores; strongly associated with coconut palm habitats and coastal human settlements. Sting is of minimal medical significance; the species is often transported inadvertently with coconut products.

children.name: Isometrus asiaticus
children.description: Distributed across southern China and Indochina; similar to I. maculatus.

children.name: Isometrus singaporensis
children.description: Common in Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia; found in urban gardens.

children.name: Lychas marmoreus
children.description: The most commonly encountered scorpion in southeastern Australia, found under bark and logs in forests and gardens. Sting is painful and may cause prolonged localised effects, but fatalities have not been recorded in healthy adults.

children.name: Lychas variatus
children.description: Widespread in eastern and southern Australia; highly variable in body patterning, hence the name. Arboreal tendencies in wooded habitats; sting causes local pain without systemic medical significance.

children.name: Lychas mucronatus
children.description: Ranging across South and Southeast Asia including India, China, and the Philippines; a strongly arboreal species concealed in tree bark and thatch. Among the more widely distributed Asian Lychas; sting is of minor medical importance.

children.name: Lychas tricarinatus
children.description: Distributed across South Asia from India to Sri Lanka and Myanmar; named for three prominent carinae on the metasomal segments. Inhabits forest and scrub land; venom of low toxicity and sting rarely causes more than localised pain.

children.name: Lychas scutilus
children.description: Occurring in peninsular India; a small, slender Lychas found in dry deciduous forest and scrubland, sheltering beneath loose bark and stones. Sting is mildly painful with no recorded fatalities; one of several poorly studied Indian Lychas species.

children.name: Lychas bihari
children.description: An Indian species from the Gangetic floodplains, commonly encountered in eastern India.

children.name: Lychas albimanus
children.description: An Australian species with pale pedipalps, found in Queensland and Northern Territory.

children.name: Ananteris
children.description: Small buthids of Central and South America with elongated metasoma; many autotomise the tail as a predator escape.

children.name: Ananteris balzani
children.description: Found in the Gran Chaco region of Paraguay and Argentina; adapted to dry thorn-scrub.

children.name: Ananteris amazonensis
children.description: A small scorpion from Amazonian rainforest floor litter; thin tail is easily autotomised.

children.name: Ananteris coarctata
children.description: Occurring from Colombia to Bolivia in leaf litter of tropical forests.

children.name: Ananteris asanae
children.description: Described from the Peruvian Amazon; named after a local conservationist.

children.name: Babycurus
children.description: Stout-bodied burrowing scorpions across sub-Saharan Africa with potent venom.

children.name: Babycurus butranti
children.description: From the savannas of Ghana and Cote d'Ivoire; constructs shallow burrows at grass bases.

children.name: Babycurus centruroides
children.description: An East African species from Kenya and Tanzania; favours rocky outcrops.

children.name: Babycurus gigas
children.description: The largest Babycurus reaching 10 cm; preys on large insects and small vertebrates.

children.name: Odontobuthus
children.description: Middle Eastern buthids with a distinctive tooth-like projection on the telson.

children.name: Odontobuthus doriae
children.description: From Iran, Iraq, and Afghanistan; common cause of envenomation on the Iranian plateau.

children.name: Odontobuthus odonturus
children.description: A Pakistani species from the Indus River basin with a distinctive ventral telson tooth.

children.name: Odontobuthus tigris
children.description: From Mesopotamian marshlands of southern Iraq; adapted to clay soils with high water tables.

children.name: Compsobuthus
children.description: Small slender buthids from North Africa through the Middle East to Pakistan.

children.name: Compsobuthus matthiesseni
children.description: Widespread from Egypt to Iran; inhabits stony desert wadis and alluvial fans.

children.name: Compsobuthus acutecarinatus
children.description: Found in Oman and Yemen; has sharply defined pedipalp carinae.
